video_title: SpaceX Hit $2 Trillion. Now Everyone Wants a Jet
gist: Israel Slottawitz (Craft CEO) maps the collision between the largest tech wealth creation event in history — SpaceX IPO + OpenAI IPO in the same week — and a private aviation market structurally unequipped to absorb it: OEM backlogs extend to 2030, 70% of buyers pay cash (limiting financing leverage), and the Craft exchange fund solves the concentrated-equity diversification problem that bonus depreciation cannot. The episode is a real-time primary market survey of how generational capital gain events translate into demand for illiquid hard assets.

themes.theme: SpaceX/OpenAI IPO Wave — Largest Single Demand Catalyst For Private Aviation in History

insights.claim: SpaceX IPO (Thursday of recording week) and OpenAI going public in the same cycle represents the largest single wealth creation event in financial history — the private aviation market is the immediate hard-asset beneficiary of this capital gain concentration.
insights.speaker: Israel Slottawitz
insights.so_what: Private aviation demand will spike in H2 2026 / 2027 from SpaceX/OpenAI equity holders who cannot absorb wealth into public markets fast enough — aircraft is the first hard asset category to see this capital, and OEM backlogs mean secondary-market aircraft prices will rise further.
insights.quote: you know this is the largest wealth creation in history... and I think you're going to see that show up in private aviation in a big way

insights.claim: Alex Karp (Palantir CEO) spent $17M on private aviation in 2025, approximately 3 months in the air — an extreme-but-real data point illustrating how ultra-high-utilization tech executives monetize time through aviation.
insights.speaker: Israel Slottawitz
insights.so_what: As AI company founders and executives monetize through IPOs, Karp-level aviation spend becomes the template — private aviation operators should model for significant HNWI demand surge in 2026-2027 correlated to tech IPO cycle.
insights.quote: Alex Karp spent $17 million on private aviation last year, he was in the air for about three months

insights.claim: Private aviation is reframed as a utility and productivity infrastructure for high-net-worth executives, not a luxury — the argument is that one critical deal or prevented mistake pays back the entire annual aviation cost, making demand inelastic to asset prices.
insights.speaker: Israel Slottawitz
insights.so_what: Demand elasticity for private aviation among the top decile of earners is very low relative to income — this supports pricing power and asset value resilience even in a risk-off environment where luxury discretionary spending contracts.
insights.quote: one deal that you do on that plane or one crisis that you help avert... that pays back your whole year of flying

themes.theme: New Tech Wealth Tax Architecture — Bonus Depreciation vs. Capital Gains Mismatch

insights.claim: Big Beautiful Bill (2025) reinstated 100% bonus depreciation on aircraft (originally introduced by 2017 TCJA) — but the benefit is structurally mismatched to new tech wealth: bonus depreciation offsets ordinary income only, and new IPO millionaires hold concentrated capital gains, not ordinary income.
insights.speaker: Israel Slottawitz
insights.so_what: The main tax lever for aircraft acquisition (bonus depreciation) is structurally useless to the largest new buyer cohort — SpaceX/OpenAI equity holders with capital gains exposure — creating demand for alternative structuring vehicles and indicating advertised tax benefits overstate effective demand stimulus.
insights.quote: bonus depreciation only works against ordinary income, not capital gains... and most of these tech people have capital gains

insights.claim: Aircraft bonus depreciation has a recapture provision: when the plane is sold, all depreciation taken is recaptured and taxed at ordinary income rates — creating a future ordinary income liability for buyers who used depreciation to offset income.
insights.speaker: Israel Slottawitz
insights.so_what: Buyers using 100% bonus depreciation on aircraft face a large deferred ordinary income liability on exit — this suppresses secondary market liquidity and skews aircraft holding periods longer; buyers sophisticated enough to model this may avoid the depreciation strategy entirely.
insights.quote: when you sell that plane, you're going to pay recapture tax at ordinary income rates on all that depreciation you took

themes.theme: Craft Exchange Fund — Structuring Innovation at the Aviation/Finance Intersection

insights.claim: Craft built an exchange fund on top of its operating aviation business: concentrated stockholders contribute stock to the fund in exchange for diversified interests, enabling tax-deferred diversification — the aviation business generates the operating cashflows needed to satisfy exchange fund qualification requirements.
insights.speaker: Israel Slottawitz
insights.so_what: Craft has created a structurally differentiated product that competitors cannot easily replicate — the fund wraps aviation operations and financial engineering together, targeting the exact unmet need of the SpaceX/OpenAI IPO cohort who have capital gains they cannot offset through aircraft depreciation.
insights.quote: we've built an exchange fund on top of the operating aviation business... so concentrated stockholders can come in and diversify

insights.claim: The exchange fund structure is specifically targeted at holders of concentrated single-stock positions (primarily pre-IPO tech equity) who want diversification without triggering a taxable capital gains event at the time of contribution.
insights.speaker: Israel Slottawitz
insights.so_what: As the SpaceX and OpenAI IPO cohorts hold locked-up shares post-IPO, Craft's exchange fund becomes directly relevant — the serviceable market for this product just expanded dramatically with the IPO cycle. Craft has first-mover advantage in the aviation-anchored exchange fund niche.
insights.quote: it's really for people who have a concentrated position they don't want to sell and don't want to pay capital gains on

themes.theme: Aircraft Supply Crunch, Unprecedented Asset Appreciation & Secondary Market

insights.claim: New aircraft OEM deliveries are booked through 2030 with 3-4 year wait times — new buyer demand from tech IPO cycle cannot be satisfied by OEM production; all demand channels to used/secondary market aircraft.
insights.speaker: Israel Slottawitz
insights.so_what: Used aircraft prices will absorb IPO demand shock entirely; secondary market operators and aircraft brokers are structurally advantaged through at least 2027-2028; OEM order slots are trading as investable assets in their own right.
insights.quote: OEMs are delivering into 2030, you're looking at three to four year wait times for a new plane

insights.claim: Challenger 350 aircraft (midsize business jet) appreciated approximately $2M above its purchase price — unprecedented in an asset class that normally depreciates from the moment of purchase, now behaving more like real estate or fine art.
insights.speaker: Israel Slottawitz
insights.so_what: The depreciation assumption baked into aircraft ownership models has inverted — buyers who purchased pre-supply crunch are sitting on unrecognized capital gains; this alters the decision calculus for selling (recapture tax on a now-appreciated asset becomes very costly).
insights.quote: the Challenger 350 we bought... it's up $2 million from when we bought it, which is completely insane

insights.claim: 70% of private aircraft are purchased in cash, with only 30% financed — highly unusual for an asset class of this size and creates a market that is structurally insulated from rising interest rate shocks relative to other hard assets.
insights.speaker: Israel Slottawitz
insights.so_what: Private aviation asset prices are more insulated from credit cycle tightening than almost any comparable hard asset class — the buyer base's wealth is not leverage-dependent, supporting price resilience in risk-off environments.
insights.quote: 70% of aircraft are bought in cash... only 30% financed, which is just wild for an asset of this size

insights.claim: OEM delivery slots are being resold in the secondary market at significant premiums as buyers unable to wait 3-4 years pay for queue position.
insights.speaker: Israel Slottawitz
insights.so_what: Delivery slot premiums represent an observable pricing signal for unmet demand intensity — as SpaceX/OpenAI IPO demand enters the market, slot premiums will widen further; brokers and operators holding slots have a new monetization opportunity.
insights.quote: people are reselling slots in the secondary market at big premiums because they just can't wait that long

insights.claim: Starlink installation costs approximately $300K on a private aircraft and has become effectively mandatory for high-end business aviation; a new disc with double the bandwidth is coming with a backlog running through 2027.
insights.speaker: Israel Slottawitz
insights.so_what: Starlink's aviation backlog through 2027 is a direct bottleneck on the productivity argument for private aviation — owners who cannot get Starlink installed face a meaningful amenity gap; SpaceX's captive revenue from aviation connectivity is underappreciated.
insights.quote: Starlink is indispensable at this point... $300K to install, and the new disc with double the bandwidth has a backlog through 2027

insights.claim: United and American Airlines signed fleet-wide Starlink deals; Delta has not — creating a measurable competitive disadvantage for Delta in attracting business class travelers who treat connectivity as a baseline requirement.
insights.speaker: Israel Slottawitz
insights.so_what: Commercial airline connectivity is bifurcating into Starlink (high bandwidth, satellite) and legacy in-flight WiFi — Delta's absence from Starlink is a churn risk for its business travelers; United and American gain a differentiation lever in premium cabin.
insights.quote: United and American have both signed fleet-wide Starlink deals... Delta has not and that's a real competitive problem for them

themes.theme: FBO Private Equity Consolidation & Market Fragmentation

insights.claim: Private equity firms have bought Fixed Base Operators (FBOs — private aviation ground service facilities) across the US; event fees at major FBOs have risen to $20K-$40K for ordinary events, where previously landing fees were minimal.
insights.speaker: Israel Slottawitz
insights.so_what: PE-driven FBO fee inflation is a margin headwind for private aviation operators and charter customers; operators who own their FBO infrastructure or have locked-in ground contracts have a structural cost advantage that will widen over time.
insights.quote: PE firms have bought up all these FBOs... and now you're seeing event fees of $20-$40K for just a normal event

insights.claim: The top 5 private aviation providers now capture 35% of all private aviation hours; the remaining 65% is fragmented among small operators with significant quality and safety variance — Argus safety ratings criticized as 'pay-for-play' and unreliable as a screening tool.
insights.speaker: Israel Slottawitz
insights.so_what: The fragmented 65% of private aviation hours represents both a market risk (quality variance) and a consolidation opportunity; any operator who can credibly solve safety/quality sorting at scale for the 65% fragmented market has a defensible competitive moat.
insights.quote: 35% of all private aviation hours go through the top five providers... the rest is fragmented, and Argus ratings? Pay for play.
